content:
# Exodus Chapter 33 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 103-104; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 76-77 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_033_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_033_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_033_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_033_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Command to Depart and the Threatened Withdrawal (vv. 1-6)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, "Go, go up from here, you and the people whom you brought up from the land of Egypt, to the land which I swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, saying, 'To your seed I will give it.'

**2** And I will send before you an angel, and I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite --

**3** to a land flowing with milk and honey; for I will not go up in your midst, for you are a stiff-necked people, lest I consume you on the way."

**4** And the people heard this evil word, and they mourned; and no man put his ornaments upon him.

**5** And YHWH said to Moses, "Say to the Sons of Israel, 'You are a stiff-necked people. One moment I could go up in your midst and I would consume you. And now, take down your ornaments from upon you, and I will know what I will do to you.'"

**6** And the Sons of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments from Mount Horeb onward.

---

## The Tent of Meeting Outside the Camp (vv. 7-11)

**7** And Moses would take the tent and pitch it outside the camp, far off from the camp, and he called it the Tent of Meeting; and it was that everyone who sought YHWH would go out to the Tent of Meeting which was outside the camp.

**8** And it was, when Moses went out to the tent, all the people would rise and stand, each man at the entrance of his tent, and they would gaze after Moses until he entered the tent.

**9** And it was, when Moses entered the tent, the pillar of cloud would descend and stand at the entrance of the tent, and he would speak with Moses.

**10** And all the people would see the pillar of cloud standing at the entrance of the tent, and all the people would rise and bow down, each man at the entrance of his tent.

**11** And YHWH would speak to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his companion; and he would return to the camp, and his attendant Joshua son of Nun, a young man, would not depart from within the tent.

---

## Moses' Plea: "Show Me Your Ways" (vv. 12-17)

**12** And Moses said to YHWH, "See, you are saying to me, 'Bring up this people,' and you have not made known to me whom you will send with me; and you, you have said, 'I know you by name,' and also, 'You have found favor in my eyes.'"

**13** "And now, if indeed I have found favor in your eyes, make known to me your ways, and I will know you, so that I may find favor in your eyes; and consider that this nation is your people."

**14** And he said, "My presence will go, and I will give you rest."

**15** And he said to him, "If your presence is not going, do not bring us up from here.

**16** And in what then will it be known that I have found favor in your eyes -- I and your people? Is it not in your going with us, and we shall be distinguished -- I and your people -- from every people that is on the face of the ground?"

**17** And YHWH said to Moses, "Also this thing that you have spoken I will do, for you have found favor in my eyes, and I know you by name."

---

## Moses' Supreme Request: "Show Me Your Glory" (vv. 18-23)

**18** And he said, "Show me, I pray, your glory."

**19** And he said, "I myself will cause all my goodness to pass before your face, and I will proclaim the name of YHWH before you; and I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and I will show compassion to whom I will show compassion."

**20** And he said, "You are not able to see my face, for no man shall see me and live."

**21** And YHWH said, "Behold, there is a place beside me, and you shall stand upon the rock.

**22** And it will be, when my glory passes by, I will put you in the cleft of the rock, and I will cover over you with my palm until I have passed by.

**23** And I will remove my palm, and you shall see my back; but my face shall not be seen."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex (B19A) presents Exodus 33 across folios 103-104. The text begins in the left column of folio 103r, continuing directly from the golden calf narrative of chapter 32. A setumah (closed paragraph break) is visible after v. 6, marking the transition from the departure command to the tent-of-meeting narrative. Another break follows v. 11, separating the tent narrative from Moses' direct petition. Marginal masorah parva notations appear beside v. 11 (the face-to-face passage). The vowel pointing and accentuation are clearly preserved throughout.

The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) presents the LXX text of Exodus 33 in the third column of page 76, continuing to page 77. The uncial script is written in scriptio continua without word divisions. The Vaticanus text closely follows the MT verse structure but shows three notable tendencies: (1) an expansion at v. 13 adding "reveal yourself to me" (absent from MT); (2) in v. 14, rendering "my face will go" as "I myself will go before you," interpreting the metonym; (3) in v. 19, inserting "glory" where MT has "goodness," harmonizing Moses' request with YHWH's response. In all cases, SOPHIA follows the MT as primary text.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

Exodus 33 is the theological pivot of the golden calf narrative (chs. 32-34). The central question is not whether Israel will reach the promised land -- that is affirmed in vv. 1-2 -- but whether YHWH himself will go with them. The chapter moves through four movements: the threatened withdrawal of presence (vv. 1-6), the mediated presence at the tent outside the camp (vv. 7-11), Moses' negotiation for restored presence (vv. 12-17), and the request for unveiled glory with its gracious limitation (vv. 18-23).

The word panim ("face/presence") is the architectural keyword of the chapter, appearing in seven distinct uses: the face-to-face dialogue (v. 11), the divine presence going with them (vv. 14-15), passing before Moses' face (v. 19), the inability to see the divine face (v. 20), and the final declaration that the face shall not be seen (v. 23). This sevenfold occurrence generates the chapter's central paradox: YHWH speaks with Moses face to face, yet his face cannot be seen. The Rabbis resolved this by distinguishing relational directness (v. 11) from the unveiled fullness of divine glory (vv. 20, 23). The text itself holds the tension without resolution.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **Habitual/iterative verbs (vv. 7-11):** All three translators recognized the yiqtol (imperfect) verbal forms as habitual or repeated action. SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in using "would" phrasing ("would take," "would rise," "would gaze," "would speak") to distinguish the iterative aspect from the simple narrative past.

2. **"Presence" for panai (v. 14):** SOPHIA renders panai as "my presence" rather than "my face" in v. 14, following LOGOS and PNEUMA. While EMET preserves "my face" for maximal literalness, "my presence" better serves the English reader by conveying the metonymic meaning (YHWH's own personal accompaniment) while the face-language is preserved elsewhere in the chapter where it refers to literal seeing (vv. 20, 23). The LXX's interpretive rendering ("I myself will go before you") confirms that the ancient translators understood panai here as a metonym for the divine person.

3. **"Goodness" not "glory" (v. 19):** All three translators correctly follow the MT's tuvi ("my goodness") against the LXX's harmonizing insertion of "glory." This is a theologically consequential decision: when Moses asks for kavod (glory, v. 18), YHWH answers with tuv (goodness, v. 19). The reframing is deliberate -- YHWH's glory is his moral character, not spectacle.

4. **"Palm" not "hand" (vv. 22-23):** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in rendering kappi as "my palm" rather than "my hand." The Hebrew kap specifically denotes the hollow of the hand or palm, not the hand generally (yad). The LXX renders with cheir ("hand"), losing this precision.

5. **"From Mount Horeb onward" (v. 6):** SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA in reading the temporal-locative phrase "from Mount Horeb onward," indicating an ongoing state of penitent austerity beginning at the mountain. LOGOS reads it as purely locative ("at Horeb"). The rabbinical consensus and context support the "onward" reading -- the stripping was not a single event but a permanent condition.

6. **Idem-per-idem construction (v. 19):** All three translators preserve the doubling structure. SOPHIA's rendering -- "I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and I will show compassion to whom I will show compassion" -- follows EMET's literalness and LOGOS's formal precision while adopting PNEUMA's "compassion" over LOGOS's "mercy" for the Piel of rakham. See disagreement log for details.

7. **"Attendant" for mesharto (v. 11):** SOPHIA follows EMET's "attendant" over LOGOS's "servant" and PNEUMA's "assistant." The Piel participle of sharat denotes ministerial service (often cultic), a more elevated register than "servant" (eved) and more precise than "assistant."
